hints.present indicative: Present indicative would be 'timetis' — 'timete' without -is is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'timeatis' — 'timete' is imperative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'timebitis' — 'timete' is present imperative

hints.teneo, tenere, tenui, tentum: These are the parts of 'tenere' (to hold)
hints.tango, tangere, tetigi, tactum: These are the parts of 'tangere' (to touch)
hints.tollo, tollere, sustuli, sublatum: These are the parts of 'tollere' (to lift/remove)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'is' — 'eum' is accusative singular masculine

steps.prompt: What case is 'qui' and what does it introduce?
steps.target: nominative relative pronoun introducing a relative clause
choices: nominative relative pronoun introducing a relative clause
steps.reveal: qui = who (nom. masc. sg., subject of 'potest')

hints.interrogative pronoun in a question: 'Qui' here refers back to 'eum' — it is relative, not interrogative
hints.indefinite pronoun: 'Qui' as indefinite usually follows 'si/nisi/ne/num' — here it is a relative pronoun
hints.conjunction meaning 'how': 'Qui' as an adverb meaning 'how' is archaic — here it is a relative pronoun

hints.to drink: 'To drink' is 'potare' — 'potest' comes from 'posse' (to be able)
hints.to place: 'To place' is 'ponere' — 'posse' means to be able
hints.to ask: 'To ask' is 'postulare' — 'posse' means to be able

steps.reveal: potest = he is able / he can

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'potuit' — 'potest' is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'possit' — 'potest' is present indicative

hints.pono, ponere, posui, positum: These are the parts of 'ponere' (to place)
hints.potior, potiri, potitus sum: These are the parts of 'potiri' (to gain possession of) — a deponent verb
hints.postulo, postulare, postulavi, postulatu: These are the parts of 'postulare' (to demand)

steps.reveal: in gehennam = into Gehenna (acc. with 'in' expressing motion)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'gehenna' — 'gehennam' with -am is accusative

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'gehennae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'gehennae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'gehennae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: The infinitive 'mittere' here is a complementary infinitive with which verb?
steps.reveal: potest...mittere = is able to send / can send

hints.'eum' (him to send): 'Eum' is a pronoun, not a verb — 'mittere' completes 'potest'

hints.moveo, movere, movi, motum: These are the parts of 'movere' (to move)
hints.misceo, miscere, miscui, mixtum: These are the parts of 'miscere' (to mix)
hints.munio, munire, munivi, munitum: These are the parts of 'munire' (to fortify)

translation: Truly I say to you: fear him who is able to cast into Gehenna
